meta.purpose: Structured benefits/selling points for the Robinson R22, R44 and R66 — for use in sales copy, detail pages, brochures and side-by-side comparisons.

conventions.headline: Short bullet-ready line (5–12 words).
conventions.body: Supporting sentence — expand the headline with a concrete fact.

entryEconomics.headline: Lowest new-build acquisition cost of any certified helicopter
entryEconomics.body: A new Beta II lists well below any competing two-seat piston — roughly half the price of a new Cabri G2.

entryEconomics.headline: Lowest direct operating cost in certified rotary aviation
entryEconomics.body: Roughly £100–£130/hr all-in, with fuel as the single largest line.

entryEconomics.headline: Fuel burn around 8–9 US gal/hr
entryEconomics.body: Less than most cars and a third of the smallest certified turbine.

entryEconomics.headline: Lowest insurance premium for student hours on type
entryEconomics.body: Underwriters price the R22 more aggressively than any other trainer because every insurer has data on it.

entryEconomics.headline: Smallest hangar footprint of any certified two-seater
entryEconomics.body: Fits diagonally inside a standard 6×6m single-car GA hangar.

entryEconomics.headline: Overhaul cost per hour lowest in class
entryEconomics.body: Lycoming O-360 overhauls are the cheapest in certified helicopter aviation.

trainingValue.headline: The default PPL(H) trainer worldwide
trainingValue.body: More hours have been logged teaching ab-initio students on the R22 than on any airframe ever built.

trainingValue.headline: Robinson Safety Course — a week at the factory
trainingValue.body: Torrance runs the only factory-taught pilot safety course in general aviation, included by most insurers.

trainingValue.headline: Low-inertia rotor teaches correct technique
trainingValue.body: Pilots trained on an R22 transition up the Robinson ladder — and to competitors — with real handling discipline.

trainingValue.headline: Instructors available at virtually every helicopter school
trainingValue.body: No planning a syllabus around instructor availability — there's always one.

trainingValue.headline: SFAR 73 compliance bakes in safety training
trainingValue.body: In the US every R22 student and instructor must meet a published, enforced training standard — the gold standard for airframe-specific training.

trainingValue.headline: Lowest-cost hour-building for commercial candidates
trainingValue.body: Nothing else comes close for the CPL(H) hour-building phase.

designAndEngineering.headline: Two-blade teetering rotor — simple by philosophy
designAndEngineering.body: Frank Robinson's design principle: every part earns its place or it's deleted.

designAndEngineering.headline: No hydraulic servos
designAndEngineering.body: Direct mechanical flight controls — nothing to leak, fail or reset.

designAndEngineering.headline: Belt-drive main transmission
designAndEngineering.body: Inspectable, replaceable, low-cost — unique in certified helicopters.

designAndEngineering.headline: Open-tube welded frame
designAndEngineering.body: Damage is visible on inspection, not hidden inside a monocoque fairing.

designAndEngineering.headline: Lycoming O-360 is the most common GA piston engine on Earth
designAndEngineering.body: Shared with Cessna 172/180s and thousands of other airframes — you are never short of parts, a workshop, or a trained engineer.

designAndEngineering.headline: Every life-limited part is published
designAndEngineering.body: No hidden calendar items, no surprises at annual.

partsAndSupport.headline: Largest parts catalogue for any piston helicopter
partsAndSupport.body: Robinson ships from Torrance direct; regional dealers stock the fast-movers.

partsAndSupport.headline: Parts in production — not NOS
partsAndSupport.body: Unlike a used Schweizer or Enstrom, every R22 part is still factory-new.

partsAndSupport.headline: Global dealer network in 70+ countries
partsAndSupport.body: Factory-trained service at almost any GA-capable airfield.

partsAndSupport.headline: Factory-run maintenance courses
partsAndSupport.body: Torrance trains engineers on the airframe directly — your shop can be factory-certified.

market.headline: 4,800+ built — the largest installed base in the class
market.body: Every major market has a used market with real depth and real liquidity.

market.headline: Resale values are published and predictable
market.body: You don't trade an R22 blind — there's comparable-sale data going back 40 years.

market.headline: You can always find a buyer
market.body: The used R22 market never dries up because the training demand never does.

missions.headline: Private owner-flyer two-seater
missions.body: The lowest-cost way to keep a helicopter current, legally and practically.

missions.headline: Agricultural / livestock mustering
missions.body: The backbone of cattle mustering in Australia, NZ, South Africa, South America.

missions.headline: Photo & film camera ship
missions.body: Cheap to operate, small enough to get into tight positions, doors-off capable.

missions.headline: Pipeline & powerline inspection
missions.body: Low operating cost makes slow, repetitive survey work commercially viable.

safety.headline: Safety Notices published openly
safety.body: Every service-learned lesson is publicly documented — no hidden failure lore.

safety.headline: Published component life limits
safety.body: Every time-limited part has a retirement hour — no calendar surprises.

safety.headline: Mature type — failure modes are catalogued
safety.body: A type-rated engineer has seen almost every fault the R22 can produce.

safety.headline: Auto-rotation teaching standard
safety.body: The R22's low rotor inertia is a feature — it forces correct entry technique that transfers to every other airframe.

points: Lower acquisition cost — typically 35–40% cheaper new.
Lower hourly operating cost.
Larger installed base (4,800+ vs ~300) — orders of magnitude more parts, instructors and data.
Resale market is mature and liquid worldwide.
SFAR 73 and Robinson Safety Course — no equivalent structured safety programme exists for the Cabri.
Lower acquisition cost.
Lower fuel burn (R22 ~8–9 gph vs ~12–14 gph).
Parts in production under a mature factory programme.
Resale market deeper than the entire Schweizer fleet.
Lower acquisition cost.
Simpler airframe (three-blade Enstrom is heavier and more complex).
Much larger support and instructor network.
Robinson still manufacturing at scale — Enstrom support has been intermittent.
Newer type, parts in production.
Lower overhaul cost.
More instructors rated on type.
Modern airframe, not a 1950s design.
Factory support — spares still rolling off the production line.
Higher cruise, longer range, lower fuel burn.

r44.name: Robinson R44
r44.tagline: The world's best-selling general aviation helicopter since 1999.
r44.powerplant: Lycoming O-540 (Raven I) or IO-540 fuel-injected (Raven II)

marketPosition.headline: The world's best-selling GA helicopter — every year since 1999
marketPosition.body: Outsells every other civilian helicopter, turbine and piston, annually.

marketPosition.headline: 6,500+ built — largest installed base of any four-seat helicopter
marketPosition.body: In every inhabited continent, at every price point, every age cohort.

marketPosition.headline: Resale is liquid at every age
marketPosition.body: You can sell a 20-year-old R44 in a weekend.

marketPosition.headline: Lease-back ready
marketPosition.body: Flight schools actively want to put paid hours on private R44s — your airframe can earn while you own it.

economics.headline: Piston operating cost with turbine mission capability
economics.body: Roughly half the hourly cost of a single turbine, with 3–4 seats of useful load.

economics.headline: Fuel burn ~15 gph in cruise
economics.body: Less fuel than a light-twin Cessna 310; far less than any comparable turbine.

economics.headline: Robinson publishes Estimated Operating Cost sheets annually
economics.body: Variant by variant — Raven I, Raven II, Cadet each have their own published figures.

economics.headline: Fixed TBO and published overhaul pricing
economics.body: Your per-hour reserve is set on day one and doesn't drift.

economics.headline: Financeable through mainstream GA lenders
economics.body: Lenders have decades of R44 loan performance — pricing and terms are standardised.

economics.headline: Insurance premiums are predictable and competitive
economics.body: Underwriters know the type, the missions, and the loss history.

missionVersatility.headline: Realistic four-seat point-to-point transport
missionVersatility.body: 350+ nm range with aux tanks; London to Edinburgh non-stop in one fuel load at Raven II speeds.

missionVersatility.headline: Training platform — PPL(H) and CPL(H)
missionVersatility.body: Cadet variant built specifically for schools.

missionVersatility.headline: Aerial tour operator workhorse
missionVersatility.body: Most small heli-tour operators worldwide run R44s.

missionVersatility.headline: ENG / news / camera ship
missionVersatility.body: Doors-off, nose-mount camera options, low noise signature, cheap to station.

missionVersatility.headline: Police patrol and observation
missionVersatility.body: Low-cost urban rotary surveillance platform — FLIR and spotlight kits available.

missionVersatility.headline: Utility / agricultural (new Utility interior, March 2026)
missionVersatility.body: Heavy-duty rubber floor, doors-off spec, cargo-ready — factory-built.

missionVersatility.headline: Amphibious via Clipper floats
missionVersatility.body: Coastal tours, island work, water rescue.

performance.headline: Raven II hot-and-high capability
performance.body: Fuel-injected IO-540 holds power at altitude and in hot conditions where carburetted piston singles fade.

performance.headline: Useful load 800–900 lb
performance.body: Four adults with bags; or two adults with a week's luggage, fuel, and reserve.

performance.headline: Cruise 110–115 kt
performance.body: Quick enough to make point-to-point travel genuinely faster than road.

maintenance.headline: Lycoming IO-540 is a standard-catalogue GA powerplant
maintenance.body: Any piston aviation shop can overhaul it — not a specialist engine.

maintenance.headline: No complex hydraulics (Raven I)
maintenance.body: Direct mechanical controls; Raven II uses simple assisted controls with no hydraulic servos to fail.

maintenance.headline: Belt-drive main transmission
maintenance.body: Inspectable, replaceable at scheduled intervals, documented.

maintenance.headline: Parts in production at full factory scale
maintenance.body: Unlike the Bell 206, you are never waiting on out-of-production spares.

partsAndSupport.headline: Robinson dealer network in 70+ countries
partsAndSupport.body: Factory-trained service almost anywhere you can land.

partsAndSupport.headline: Parts shipped from Torrance next-day globally
partsAndSupport.body: Robinson holds inventory specifically for AOG response.

partsAndSupport.headline: Engineer training delivered at the factory
partsAndSupport.body: Shops can send staff to Torrance to certify directly on the airframe.

pilotEcosystem.headline: More R44-rated pilots available to hire than any comparable type
Robinson Safety Course for R44 offered worldwide
pilotEcosystem.body: Insurance-recognised, factory-delivered — not a third-party substitute.

pilotEcosystem.headline: Transition from R22 is straightforward
pilotEcosystem.body: Most R22-rated pilots convert to R44 in a few hours.

heritage.headline: Frank Robinson's definitive airframe
heritage.body: The fullest expression of simple, dependable, affordable design.

heritage.headline: Circumnavigated the globe — twice
heritage.body: Jennifer Murray crew 1997; Murray solo 2000 (Guinness World Record, age 60).

heritage.headline: Reached both poles under piston power
heritage.body: Smith & Brooks: North Pole 2002, South Pole 2005 — the first piston helicopter ever to do either, and the only piston helicopter to do both.

heritage.headline: Decades of service data
heritage.body: Every variant, every mission, every climate — all documented.

safety.headline: Safety Notices published openly and kept current
safety.body: Every service-learned issue is in the open, not concealed by the factory.

safety.headline: Published component life limits
safety.body: Every time-limited part has a retirement hour — no calendar mystery.

safety.headline: SFAR 73 / Robinson Safety Course
safety.body: The only airframe-specific safety training programme enforced by regulation and insurers.

points: Four seats instead of two.
Real useful range (350+ nm vs ~200 nm).
110-115 kt cruise vs ~96 kt.
Hot-and-high capability (Raven II) that the R22 cannot match.
Still piston-cheap to operate.
Roughly half the acquisition cost.
Roughly half the fuel burn.
Piston simplicity — any GA piston shop can work on it.
AvGas 100LL vs Jet-A — irrelevant for most private operators, decisive in some regions.
Overhaul cost roughly one-third that of a turbine.
Lower insurance premium.
Piston operating cost vs turbine.
Parts in production at full factory scale — not NOS or substitutes.
Modern cockpit options available.
Factory support and safety programme active and funded.
Lower acquisition cost even on the used market for comparable age.
Four seats vs two — a different mission class entirely.
Longer range, higher cruise, greater payload.
Massively larger support, parts and pilot ecosystem.
Deeper resale market.
Piston (Raven II) runs roughly half the Enstrom 480B turbine's operating cost.
Larger global support network.
Deeper resale market.
Robinson's factory safety course vs no structured equivalent.
Piston economics vs turbine.
Parts still in production.
Cheaper to acquire and operate by a wide margin.
Simpler systems.
R44 is piston (cheaper to run) and 4-seat with a usable mission profile.
Factory still in production — Schweizer/Sikorsky turbine support has waned.
Deep used market vs a shrinking one.

turbineAdvantages.headline: Rolls-Royce RR300 — flat-rated, overbuilt, industry-proven
turbineAdvantages.body: Delivers full rated power deep into hot and high conditions; derated in cruise for long component life.

turbineAdvantages.headline: Jet-A available globally
turbineAdvantages.body: Far more widely stocked than AvGas 100LL — a decisive advantage in Africa, Asia, Latin America, and at most international airports.

turbineAdvantages.headline: No mixture, no carb heat, no shock cooling
turbineAdvantages.body: Simpler pilot workload, fewer failure modes, no mismanagement risk on start-up or descent.

turbineAdvantages.headline: Smooth and quiet cabin
turbineAdvantages.body: Turbine character — no engine vibration, no exhaust pulse.

turbineAdvantages.headline: Longer component lives than piston equivalents
turbineAdvantages.body: Fewer reciprocating parts; overhaul scope is contained and standardised.

missionCapability.headline: Five seats with real useful load
missionCapability.body: Pilot and four passengers with fuel and baggage at density altitudes a piston single can't clear.

missionCapability.headline: Lockable, external-access cargo hold
missionCapability.body: 300 lb capacity behind the cabin — unique in this class; R66 is the only light single with a real bag hold.

missionCapability.headline: Single-pilot IFR capable (R66 IFR)
missionCapability.body: Not just IFR-legal — certified for single-pilot operation in IMC.

missionCapability.headline: Garmin G500H TXi flight display standard
missionCapability.body: Synthetic vision, integrated engine monitoring, touchscreen navigation.

missionCapability.headline: Cargo hook for external load
missionCapability.body: Construction, fire-fighting, utility lift missions supported from the factory.

missionCapability.headline: Factory Utility / Hi-Skid variant
missionCapability.body: Long skids, cargo hook, crew shuttle configuration available new.

missionCapability.headline: Turbine Marine variant for coastal and island work

economics.headline: Cheapest certified single-engine turbine on the new market
economics.body: Roughly one-third the acquisition cost of a new H125.

economics.headline: Operating cost roughly half an Airbus H125 / AStar
economics.body: A third of an EC135 twin — on a similar owner-flyer mission profile.

economics.headline: Robinson publishes annual Estimated Operating Cost sheets for R66
economics.body: Same transparency as the piston line — variant-specific, updated yearly.

economics.headline: Financeable through mainstream GA lenders
economics.body: R66 has been in service long enough that lenders have loss data and standard terms.

economics.headline: Strong resale for a light turbine
economics.body: Installed base now deep enough that R66 used values are predictable.

economics.headline: Insurance priced comparably to R44 hull
economics.body: The simpler R66 systems and mature operating record keep turbine premiums realistic.

performance.headline: 125 kt cruise speed
performance.body: 15–20 kt faster than an R44, 40 kt faster than an R22 — point-to-point flight starts making real sense.

performance.headline: 14,000 ft service ceiling with real performance at altitude
performance.body: Unlike a piston single, the R66 doesn't become an anchor at density altitude.

performance.headline: Useful load ~1,200 lb
performance.body: Pilot, four passengers, baggage, and a full fuel load — all in.

performance.headline: Strong hot-and-high performance
performance.body: Flat-rated turbine delivers rated power deep into summer density altitudes.

systemsAndCockpit.headline: Garmin G500H TXi PFD/MFD standard
systemsAndCockpit.body: Touchscreen primary and multifunction displays, synthetic vision.

systemsAndCockpit.headline: Digital engine monitoring and FADEC-like engine management
systemsAndCockpit.body: Engine limits enforced automatically; no pilot overspeed/overtemp risk in routine ops.

systemsAndCockpit.headline: Low switch count and clean panel by design
systemsAndCockpit.body: Robinson's simplicity philosophy preserved in the turbine.

systemsAndCockpit.headline: Moving-map, TAWS, ADS-B integrated from factory

ownershipAndSupport.headline: Robinson factory support for every R66 worldwide
ownershipAndSupport.body: Torrance runs warranty, parts, engineer training and safety programmes directly.

ownershipAndSupport.headline: Rolls-Royce RR300 is a supported, maturing powerplant
ownershipAndSupport.body: Approved overhaul facilities in the US, Europe and Asia; engine parts in production.

ownershipAndSupport.headline: Robinson dealer network in 70+ countries
ownershipAndSupport.body: Same support footprint as the piston line — rare for a light turbine.

ownershipAndSupport.headline: Factory training in Torrance for pilots and engineers
ownershipAndSupport.body: Safety Course includes R66-specific syllabus; maintenance course covers turbine airframe.

marketPosition.headline: 1,500+ built — largest installed base of its class
marketPosition.body: No other sub-$2M turbine single has this scale.

marketPosition.headline: Used market exists and is liquid
marketPosition.body: A 10-year-old R66 is no longer a gamble on resale.

marketPosition.headline: Flagship of the Robinson range
marketPosition.body: The visible top of the ladder for owners and operators who have graduated through R22 and R44.

safety.headline: Robinson Safety Course, R66-specific
safety.body: Factory-delivered, insurance-recognised, includes turbine operation syllabus.

safety.headline: Turbine engine reliability
safety.body: Fewer reciprocating parts, no carb heat management, no shock cooling.

safety.headline: Published component life limits and Safety Notices
safety.body: Same open-data philosophy as R22/R44 — nothing hidden.

safety.headline: Single-pilot IFR option
safety.body: A capability most light turbines of this price cannot match.

safety.headline: Mature type — 15+ years in service
safety.body: Known failure modes, documented maintenance experience, type-rated engineers everywhere.

points: Turbine reliability — fewer moving parts, no shock-cooling, no mixture management.
Five seats vs four.
Real lockable cargo hold — not just foot-wells.
Jet-A globally available; AvGas 100LL increasingly scarce.
Single-pilot IFR option — unavailable on R44.
Hot/high performance the R44 cannot deliver at loaded weights.
Lower acquisition cost.
Lower operating cost.
Significantly larger global support network.
Simpler systems, fewer failure modes.
Factory safety course vs no equivalent.
Deeper used market for resale.
Roughly one-third the acquisition cost.
Roughly half the operating cost.
Similar five-seat mission profile for owner-flyers.
Simpler maintenance — fewer specialist items.
Lower insurance premium.
Roughly one-quarter the acquisition cost.
Roughly a third the operating cost.
Far simpler systems — lower pilot workload.
Mission profile suits private/owner-flyer use equally well.
Newer type with factory support programme.
Parts in production at factory scale.
Single-pilot IFR option available (R66 IFR).
Larger global engineer network.
Lower operating cost.
Newer airframe with a factory still producing it.
Modern glass cockpit standard.
Parts in production — no NOS pricing.
Factory support active, safety course running, dealer network current.
Better dispatch reliability (parts flow and warranty coverage).
Larger cabin and baggage capability.
Significantly larger global support and pilot network.
Robinson factory-delivered safety programme.
Stronger resale market.
Mature type with broader service history.
